capabilities_used: sub (3 subscribers, 2 healthy + 1 wedged via raw socat)
ls/cat introspection of topics/ + deferred/ + drops_*
ps-based broker descendant counting over time
shipped delay path via idle-cpu probe
capabilities_attempted_but_failed: forcing drops_<wedged> to rise via a NAIVE sequential pub loop (impossible: pub is ~1 msg/s, buffer never fills)
completing a large single-stream flood (each pub blocks ~1s linger -> minutes-long run -> host kills container, exit 137)
evidence_log: PROBE: `docker run ... bash /work/.eval-scratch/flooder-probe.sh` -> bash 5.2; SUB registers `topics/chan/sub_<pid>.fifo`; `serve` with bad arg prints raw `/work/src/shellmux: line 248: $1: unbound variable` (no usage).
CONTROL nowedge flood (flooder-attack5.sh nowedge 120): both healthy subs got 120/120, EXIT=0; monitor: tot procs 15->27 steady, 0 timeout-writers, free_mb ~7200 flat.

WEDGE flood 120 small msgs (flooder-attack5.sh wedge 120): 3 subs, both healthy 120/120, EXIT=0, BUT `(no drops_* files present)` -- 120 tiny msgs never fill the wedged buffer; timeout-writers peaked at 2, tot peaked 32 (vs 27 control) -> bounded.

DIAGNOSIS of repeated EXIT=137: TIMEBOXED run (flooder-timeboxed.sh wedge 30 2000) -> `published=30 in ~30s -> rate 1.0 msg/s`, every pub `dur` ~1.018s, all rc=0. The earlier OOM-137s were my long sequential floods (1 msg/s * 1500 = ~25min) being killed by the host, NOT a broker leak.

PARALLEL conc=10 flood (flooder-parallel.sh wedge 400 10 4000): monitor shows broker `desc` plateau at 33 with `timeout=10` writers (176 samples), then recede to baseline 13 (909 samples); `tot` max 99, `free_mb` min 7143 -> descendants track in-flight concurrency, do NOT accumulate per-message.

PARALLEL conc=40 flood: broker `desc` peaked 96 with `timeout=40` (== my CONC), `free_mb` min 6724, then receded to 13 in the tail samples -> bounded by concurrency, recovers; host killed my client fork storm (tot=309) = harness limit, not broker.

SHIPPED tests/flood_wedged.sh RUN #1: `FAIL F1 healthy subs did not fully drain within 60s (h1=992 h2=992 of 1000)`; ok F2 drops=395; ok F3 max-broker-descendants=18; ok F4 frames intact; `result: pass=3 fail=1`, EXIT=1.

SHIPPED tests/flood_wedged.sh RUN #2 (identical cmd): `ok F1 ... received the ENTIRE flood ... 1000/1000 in ~3s`; F2 drops=346; F3 max-descendants=19; F4 ok; `result: pass=4 fail=0`, EXIT=0 -> SAME TEST, SAME CODE, FLIPPED OUTCOME (60s/992 vs 3s/1000).

IDLE CPU probe (idle-cpu.sh): deferred file `1782181134146.2246018654` appears in deferred/; `utime delta=0 stime delta=0` over 5s idle (~0% scheduler CPU); message `reboot-now` delivered and deferred/ emptied (consumed).
what_worked_well: The no-leak / flat-process claim (the literal heart of this challenge) HOLDS hard: across conc=10 (desc plateau 33), conc=40 (peak 96), and the shipped test (max_descendants 18-19), broker descendants track IN-FLIGHT concurrency and recede to baseline 13 -- never per-message accumulation. The old `printf > $f &` leak is genuinely gone.
Backpressure overflow is VISIBLE, not silent: `cat topics/chan/drops_*` shows real counts (346, 395) under sufficient pressure -- exactly as advertised.
Frame integrity under flood is solid (shipped F4 ok both runs): no torn/concatenated records even while the wedged peer overflows.
Introspection is genuinely just the filesystem: `ls topics/`, `ls topics/<t>/sub_*.fifo`, `cat drops_*`, `ls deferred/` all work and tell the truth.
Scheduler idle CPU is real ~0% (0 utime+stime ticks over 5s while a --delay 8 was pending), and the deferred fire delivered correctly and consumed its `deferred/<run_at>.<seq>` file.
wished_for: A `pub --no-linger` / `--fire-and-forget` flag (or a documented batch/streaming publish) so a user can actually flood without ~1s-per-message blocking and a fork of the whole CLI per message.

DEMO.md Beat 3 to tell the user HOW to flood (background the pubs / use the shipped test) and to stop claiming healthy subs get 'the ENTIRE flood at full speed' -- the shipped test itself disagrees ~half the time.

A deterministic flood test (or a documented tolerance) so a live demo of Beat 3 can't fail in front of a judge, plus a clean usage/error message for `serve` instead of `line 248: $1: unbound variable`.

A `drops_<pid>` file that exists at 0 from subscriber registration (currently it only appears after the first drop), so a user watching `cat drops_<wedged>` doesn't stare at a missing file wondering if anything is wired up.

friction_points.description: The author's OWN shipped tests/flood_wedged.sh is non-deterministic for the healthy-subscriber criterion. Identical command, identical code: run #1 = FAIL F1 (healthy subs 992/1000, ~60s), exit 1; run #2 = ok F1 (1000/1000, ~3s), exit 0. The 'healthy subscribers keep receiving in real time' success criterion is only intermittently met, and the gating test could fail live in a demo.
friction_points.evidence: RUN1: 'FAIL F1 healthy subs did not fully drain within 60s (h1=992 h2=992 of 1000)' result pass=3 fail=1 EXIT=1. RUN2 (same cmd): 'ok F1 ... 1000/1000 in ~3s' result pass=4 fail=0 EXIT=0.
friction_points.workaround: Re-run the test until it passes (unacceptable for a proof). No user-side fix.

friction_points.description: DEMO.md Beat 3 claims 'The two healthy subscribers get the ENTIRE flood at full speed' and '~15' procs. The shipped test contradicts the 'entire flood' part ~half the time (992/1000), and the real bounded ceiling under concurrency is higher (desc 33 at conc=10, 96 at conc=40, max_descendants 18-19 in the shipped test).
friction_points.evidence: DEMO.md:71-74 vs shipped F1 fail (992/1000) and my conc trajectories (desc 33/96).
friction_points.workaround: Treat Beat 3's 'entire flood' as best-effort, not guaranteed.

friction_points.description: `pub` blocks ~1.0s per call (the linger) and forks the whole CLI+socat per message. A naive sequential flooder achieves ~1 msg/s, so the wedged buffer NEVER fills and drops_<wedged> NEVER rises -- the exact thing DEMO.md tells the user to watch. You must background pubs (a fork storm) or use the hidden shipped test; nothing in README/DEMO says so.
friction_points.evidence: flooder-timeboxed.sh: 'published=30 in ~30s -> rate 1.0 msg/s', pub dur median 1.018s; wedge flood of 120 small msgs produced '(no drops_* files present)'.
friction_points.workaround: Background pubs with bounded concurrency, or run tests/flood_wedged.sh.

friction_points.description: `shellmux serve` with a missing/extra arg prints a raw bash error `line 248: $1: unbound variable` instead of a usage message. A user probing the CLI gets internals, not help.
friction_points.workaround: Provide all expected args; consult DEMO.md for the exact invocation.

friction_points.description: Several of my longer flood scripts died with exit 137. Root-caused (NOT a broker leak): the ~1s pub linger makes any sizeable sequential flood run for many minutes and the host kills the container; the broker descendant count and free memory stayed flat the entire time (monitor logs persisted to the mount confirm desc<=18, free_mb>~7GB right up to the kill).
friction_points.evidence: hard-wedge monitor: 3693 samples over ~7min, tot steady 35, desc 15, free_mb 7158 flat, no FLOOD_END marker -> run was killed mid-flood, broker never degraded.
friction_points.workaround: Use parallel bounded pubs or the shipped test; don't sequential-flood thousands.

proof_assessment.challenge_core_claim: writers do not accumulate per-message under a wedged-subscriber flood
proof_assessment.broker_descendant_behavior: tracks in-flight publisher concurrency (timeout-writers == my CONC), plateaus, then recedes to baseline 13; never grows with cumulative message count
supporting_evidence: shipped F3 max_descendants 18-19 across two runs
my conc=10 plateau desc=33 then recede
my conc=40 peak desc=96 then recede
proof_assessment.idle_cpu_claim: PROVEN (~0%: 0 utime+stime ticks over 5s with a --delay pending)
proof_assessment.healthy_subs_realtime_claim: FLAKY/NOT-RELIABLY-PROVEN: shipped F1 failed (992/1000, 60s) on one run, passed (1000/1000, 3s) on an immediate re-run with no changes
proof_assessment.note: This challenge does not touch the chaos/delay/negative-control proof; the deadline scheduler's missed=0/dup=0 with must-fail controls was out of scope and not re-audited here (only the idle-CPU + single delay fire were spot-checked and held).

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md Beat 3: 'the two healthy subscribers get the ENTIRE flood at full speed'
claim_audit.evidence: shipped tests/flood_wedged.sh run #1: F1 FAIL, healthy subs received 992/1000 in ~60s, exit 1. Re-run passed. Non-deterministic.

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md Beat 3: 'cat drops_<wedged> ticks up (lossy, but visible -- never silent)'
claim_audit.evidence: shipped F2 drops sum 346/395; cat topics/chan/drops_* returns counts. (Caveat: only when flood rate is high enough to fill the buffer -- not at ~1 msg/s sequential pub.)

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md Beat 3: 'the publisher's process count stays flat (~15)' / 'no per-message process accumulation'
claim_audit.evidence: shipped F3 max_descendants 18-19; my conc-scaled plateaus (33 at conc=10, 96 at conc=40) recede to baseline 13; free memory flat. Ceiling tracks concurrency, not message count; '~15' is right only at low concurrency.

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md/README: '~0% idle CPU' for the scheduler during a delay
claim_audit.evidence: idle-cpu.sh: utime delta=0 stime delta=0 over 5s while --delay 8 pending.

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md: '--delay fires and lands; deferred file is <run_at_ms>.<seq>'
claim_audit.evidence: deferred/1782181134146.2246018654 appeared then was consumed; 'reboot-now' delivered to the subscriber.

claim_audit.claim: README/DEMO implied: a user can 'flood the topic' with pub
claim_audit.evidence: pub blocks ~1.0s/call (linger) -> ~1 msg/s sequential; flooding requires backgrounding pubs or the shipped test, undocumented.

errors_encountered.error: exit 137 (OOM/host-kill) on multiple long flood scripts
errors_encountered.root_cause: NOT the broker -- the ~1s pub linger made sequential floods run for minutes; the broker's procs+memory stayed flat per persisted monitor logs
errors_encountered.resolution: switched to time-boxed and bounded-concurrency floods; root-caused via flooder-timeboxed.sh showing 1.0 msg/s

errors_encountered.root_cause: minimal/faithful dep set has no bc

errors_encountered.resolution: supplied full args; logged as friction

free_text:
As the adversarial flooder I came to break the isolation/no-leak claim, and on that specific axis the project WINS: I could not make broker descendant processes accumulate per-message. Under a wedged subscriber the descendant count tracks how many publishes are in flight (timeout-writers == my concurrency cap), plateaus, and recedes to baseline 13 -- conc=10 plateaued at desc=33, conc=40 peaked at 96, the shipped test capped at 18-19, and free memory never dipped below ~6.7GB. drops_<wedged> rises and is visible via cat, frames stay intact, scheduler idle CPU is genuinely ~0%. The old `printf > $f &` leak is dead. Good work, and I tried hard to defeat it.

BUT the OTHER half of THIS challenge -- 'healthy subscribers keep receiving in real time' -- is not reliably true, and the proof is the author's OWN test eating itself: I ran tests/flood_wedged.sh twice with zero changes. Run 1: FAIL F1, healthy subs got 992/1000 over ~60s, exit 1. Run 2: pass, 1000/1000 in ~3s, exit 0. A 20x latency swing and 8 lost messages on a coin-flip. That is a flaky gate on a headline supporting claim -- it could fail live in front of a judge, and DEMO.md's 'the two healthy subscribers get the ENTIRE flood at full speed' is therefore an overclaim.

Usability landmine for any real flooder: `pub` blocks ~1 full second per call (linger) and forks the whole CLI per message. Follow DEMO.md literally -- loop `pub` to 'flood the topic' and 'watch drops_<wedged> tick up' -- and you get ~1 msg/s, the wedged buffer never fills, and NO drops file ever appears. To actually reproduce Beat 3 you must background pubs (a fork storm) or know to run the hidden shipped test; nothing in README/DEMO says so. Net: the leak claim is earned and solid; the real-time-healthy-delivery claim is flaky and the manual flood recipe in the docs does not work as written.
